1:
"And of the blue, and purple, and scarlet, they made cloths of service, to do service in the holy place, and made the holy garments for Aaron; as the LORD commanded Moses." The witnesses note the cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron made of the rich colours, as the LORD commanded. Gill notes Jarchi observes there is no mention of linen here, because these were not the priestly garments in which there was linen, but the cloths of service in which the holy things were wrapped. The witnesses note the making of cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron, of blue, purple, and scarlet, to do service in the holy place, as the LORD commanded Moses; the executing of the priestly vestments after the pattern — that the garments commanded are now made: "of the blue, and purple, and scarlet, they made cloths of service... and made the holy garments for Aaron; as the LORD commanded Moses"; the witnesses note the cloths of service, that these, lacking linen, were not the linen "priestly garments" but the cloths "in which the holy things were wrapped" for carriage; the witnesses note the holy garments, "the holy garments for Aaron" — the high priest's glorious vestments, now wrought; the witnesses note the rule, "as the LORD commanded Moses" — that all was done exactly as commanded, a refrain repeated through the chapter; the witnesses note the type before given, that the holy garments shadow the righteousness and graces with which Christ, our High Priest, and his people are clothed for God's service; the witnesses note that the making answered the command, the work done after the pattern; so that the garments are made as the LORD commanded; as the LORD commanded Moses. The witnesses note of the blue, and purple, and scarlet, they made cloths of service, to do service in the holy place, and made the holy garments for Aaron. The witnesses note as the LORD commanded Moses.

The witnesses note the cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron made of the rich colours, all "as the LORD commanded Moses" — a refrain repeated through the chapter; the holy garments shadowing the righteousness and graces with which Christ our High Priest and his people are clothed for God's service, the work done exactly after the pattern.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the priestly vestments after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the garments were made as the LORD commanded, a type of being clothed with righteousness for God's service. The witnesses note the cloths of service: "they made cloths of service" — Gill notes for wrapping the holy things; the cloths of service; the for wrapping; the the holy things; the no linen; the carriage. The expositors observe the holy garments: "the holy garments for Aaron" — the glorious vestments; the holy garments; the the glorious vestments; the the high priest; the wrought; the rich. The witnesses press the as commanded: "as the LORD commanded Moses" — exactly; the as commanded; the exactly; the the refrain; the the pattern; the obeyed. The expositors draw the lesson on the righteousness: a type of righteousness; the righteousness; the righteousness; the the graces; the clothed; the for service. The expositors note the answered the command: the work answered the command. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as the LORD commanded Moses, and the garments. The verse thus records the making of the garments — teaching that they were made as the LORD commanded, a type of being clothed with righteousness.
2:
"And he made the ephod of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen." The witnesses note the ephod made of gold, the colours, and fine twined linen. Gill notes that from here to the end of verse thirty-one is a very particular account of the making of the priest's garments, exactly according to the directions given to Moses. The witnesses note the making of the ephod of gold, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen; the executing of the ephod, the chief garment of the high priest, after the pattern — that the ephod commanded is now made: "he made the ephod of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen"; the witnesses note that from this verse the account gives "a very particular account of the making of the priest's garments, exactly according to the directions given to Moses" (ch. 28); the witnesses note the ephod, "the ephod of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen" — the chief and most glorious vestment, woven of the precious metal and the rich colours and linen; the witnesses note that the gold was wrought in (v.3), the colours and linen woven together with cunning work; the witnesses note the type before given, that the ephod, the high priest's chief garment, on which the names of the tribes were borne (vv.6-7), shadows Christ, our High Priest, who bears his people before God; the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly, the work done after the pattern; the witnesses note that the glory of the garment befitted the dignity of the office; so that the ephod is made after the pattern; and fine twined linen. The witnesses note And he made the ephod of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet. The witnesses note and fine twined linen.

The witnesses note the ephod, the chief garment of the high priest, made of gold, the rich colours, and fine twined linen, exactly according to the directions given to Moses — a very particular record of the making; the ephod, on which the names of the tribes were borne, shadowing Christ our High Priest, who bears his people before God.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the ephod after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the ephod, the chief vestment, was made exactly as directed, a type of Christ the High Priest who bears his people. The witnesses note the the ephod: "the ephod of gold" — Gill notes a particular account; the the ephod; the a particular account; the the chief garment; the the directions; the as Moses. The expositors observe the gold and colours: "gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet" — the precious materials; the gold and colours; the the precious materials; the the gold; the the colours; the the linen. The witnesses press the woven together: woven with cunning work; the woven together; the woven; the cunning work; the the gold wrought in; the the colours. The expositors draw the lesson on the bears his people: the high priest bears his people; the bears his people; the the high priest; the bears; the his people; the before God. The witnesses observe the type of Christ: a type of Christ; the type of Christ; the Christ; the the High Priest; the his people; the borne. The expositors note the answered the command: the work answered the command. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and fine twined linen, and the ephod. The verse thus records the making of the ephod — teaching that it was made exactly as directed, a type of Christ who bears his people.
3:
"And they did beat the gold into thin plates, and cut it into wires, to work it in the blue, and in the purple, and in the scarlet, and in the fine linen, with cunning work." The witnesses note the gold beaten into thin plates and cut into wires to be worked into the ephod. Adam Clarke notes they beat the gold into thin plates for the purpose, as supposed, of cutting it into wires or threads, to twist or twine being the common acceptation of the root. The witnesses note the gold beaten into thin plates and cut into wires, to be worked with cunning into the blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen; the curious working of the gold into the very fabric of the ephod — that the gold is wrought as thread into the garment: "they did beat the gold into thin plates, and cut it into wires, to work it in the blue, and in the purple, and in the scarlet, and in the fine linen, with cunning work"; the witnesses note the working, "beat the gold into thin plates, and cut it into wires" — that the gold was hammered thin and cut into fine "wires" or threads, "to twist or twine"; the witnesses note the use, "to work it in the blue... purple... scarlet... fine linen, with cunning work" — that these golden threads were woven into the colours and linen, the gold running through the whole; the witnesses note the skill and labour, that this was a work of great art and care, the gold curiously interwoven; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the gold wrought through the whole garment may shadow the graces interwoven in the righteousness with which the saints are clothed; the witnesses note that the work of God's house called for the finest art and the costliest stuff; so that the gold is wrought as thread into the ephod; with cunning work. The witnesses note they did beat the gold into thin plates, and cut it into wires, to work it in the blue, and in the purple, and in the scarlet, and in the fine linen. The witnesses note with cunning work.

The witnesses note the gold beaten thin and cut into fine wires or threads, woven with cunning work into the colours and linen of the ephod — the gold running through the whole, a work of great art and care; some seeing the gold wrought through the garment shadow the graces interwoven in the righteousness with which the saints are clothed.

So the witnesses make the verse the curious working of the gold into the ephod. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gold was beaten and cut into threads and woven through the whole garment, a work of fine art, some seeing the graces interwoven in the saints' righteousness. The witnesses note the beat into plates: "beat the gold into thin plates" — Clarke notes hammered thin; the beat into plates; the hammered thin; the cut into wires; the threads; the to twine. The expositors observe the work it in: "to work it in the blue... linen" — woven; the work it in; the woven; the the colours; the the linen; the through the whole. The witnesses press the cunning work: "with cunning work" — great art; the curiously. The expositors draw the lesson on the gold through the whole: the gold runs through the whole; the gold through the whole; the the gold; the through; the the whole; the the garment. The expositors note the finest art: the finest art and costliest stuff. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the with cunning work, and the gold. The verse thus records the working of the gold — teaching that it was wrought as thread through the whole ephod, a work of fine art.
6:
"And they wrought onyx stones inclosed in ouches of gold, graven, as signets are graven, with the names of the children of Israel." The witnesses note the onyx stones set in gold, engraved with the names of the children of Israel. Adam Clarke notes the onyx stones, possibly the Egyptian pebble, set in gold and graven with the names of the tribes, as before directed. The witnesses note the making of the onyx stones, enclosed in ouches of gold and engraved, like a signet, with the names of the children of Israel; the executing of the shoulder-stones bearing the tribes' names after the pattern — that the names of Israel are graven on the stones: "they wrought onyx stones inclosed in ouches of gold, graven, as signets are graven, with the names of the children of Israel"; the witnesses note the stones, "onyx stones inclosed in ouches of gold" — two precious stones, set in gold settings, made as commanded (28:9-11); the witnesses note the engraving, "graven, as signets are graven, with the names of the children of Israel" — that the names of the twelve tribes were cut into the stones, as a seal is cut, with deep and lasting workmanship; the witnesses note the great type before given, that these stones, with the tribes' names, set on the shoulders of the ephod (v.7), shadow Christ, our High Priest, who bears the names of his people graven, sure and lasting, and carries them on his shoulders, the place of strength; the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly; the witnesses note that the names of God's people are dear to him and borne by their High Priest; so that the names are graven on the onyx stones; with the names of the children of Israel. The witnesses note And they wrought onyx stones inclosed in ouches of gold, graven, as signets are graven. The witnesses note with the names of the children of Israel.

The witnesses note the onyx stones set in gold and engraved, like a signet, with the names of the tribes — deep and lasting workmanship; the great type that these stones, set on the shoulders of the ephod, shadow Christ our High Priest, who bears the names of his people graven, sure and lasting, on his shoulders, the place of strength, the names of God's people dear to him.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the shoulder-stones bearing the tribes' names. The lesson the expositors draw is that the names of Israel were graven on the onyx stones, a type of Christ who bears his people's names sure and lasting on his shoulders. The witnesses note the onyx stones: "onyx stones inclosed in ouches of gold" — Clarke notes as commanded; the onyx stones; the as commanded; the set in gold; the the settings; the two stones. The expositors observe the graven as signets: "graven, as signets are graven" — deep and lasting; the graven as signets; the deep and lasting; the like a seal; the cut; the workmanship. The witnesses press the the names: "with the names of the children of Israel" — the tribes; the the names; the the tribes; the the twelve; the graven; the borne. The expositors draw the lesson on the bears on his shoulders: borne on the shoulders; the bears on his shoulders; the borne; the the shoulders; the the place of strength; the carried. The expositors note the dear to him: God's people dear to him, borne by their High Priest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the with the names of the children of Israel, and the stones. The verse thus records the shoulder-stones — teaching that the tribes' names were graven on them, a type of Christ who bears his people on his shoulders.
7:
"And he put them on the shoulders of the ephod, that they should be stones for a memorial to the children of Israel; as the LORD commanded Moses." The witnesses note the stones set on the shoulders of the ephod, for a memorial of the children of Israel. Charles Ellicott notes the stones for a memorial, as before directed (Exodus 28:12), that the high priest bore the names of the tribes before the LORD continually. The witnesses note the stones set on the shoulders of the ephod, to be a memorial of the children of Israel, as the LORD commanded Moses; the placing of the names on the shoulders, the high priest bearing his people in remembrance before God — that the tribes are borne for a memorial: "he put them on the shoulders of the ephod, that they should be stones for a memorial to the children of Israel"; the witnesses note the placing, "on the shoulders of the ephod" — that the two stones, with the tribes' names, were set on the two shoulder-pieces; the witnesses note the purpose, "stones for a memorial to the children of Israel" — that the high priest, wearing them, bore the names of all the tribes "before the LORD continually," for a memorial, that God might remember his people; the witnesses note the rule, "as the LORD commanded Moses" — the refrain of exact obedience; the witnesses note the great type before given, that Christ, our High Priest, bears his people on his shoulders, in his strength, presenting them and their names before God for a perpetual memorial; the witnesses note that we are remembered before God by our High Priest, borne on his strength; the witnesses note that the making answered the command; so that the names are borne on the shoulders for a memorial; as the LORD commanded Moses. The witnesses note he put them on the shoulders of the ephod, that they should be stones for a memorial to the children of Israel. The witnesses note as the LORD commanded Moses.

The witnesses note the stones with the tribes' names set on the shoulders of the ephod, a memorial of the children of Israel, as the LORD commanded — the high priest bearing his people before the LORD continually; a type of Christ our High Priest, who bears his people on his shoulders, in his strength, presenting them before God for a perpetual memorial.

So the witnesses make the verse the placing of the names on the shoulders, the high priest bearing his people in remembrance. The lesson the expositors draw is that the tribes were borne for a memorial before God, a type of Christ who bears his people on his shoulders for a perpetual memorial. The witnesses note the on the shoulders: "on the shoulders of the ephod" — Ellicott notes the shoulder-pieces; the on the shoulders; the the shoulder-pieces; the the two stones; the set; the the names. The expositors observe the a memorial: "stones for a memorial" — before the LORD; the a memorial; the before the LORD; the continually; the remembered; the the tribes. The witnesses press the as commanded: "as the LORD commanded Moses" — exact obedience; the as commanded; the exact obedience; the the refrain; the the pattern; the obeyed. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ bears them: Christ bears his people; the Christ bears them; the Christ; the bears; the his people; the his shoulders. The expositors note the perpetual memorial: a perpetual memorial, remembered by our High Priest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as the LORD commanded Moses, and the memorial. The verse thus records the names on the shoulders — teaching that the tribes were borne for a memorial, a type of Christ who bears his people on his shoulders.
8:
"And he made the breastplate of cunning work, like the work of the ephod; of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen." The witnesses note the breastplate made of cunning work, like the ephod, of gold and the rich colours. Adam Clarke notes the breastplate, made as before directed (Exodus 28:15), of cunning work, like the work of the ephod. The witnesses note the making of the breastplate, of cunning work like the ephod, of gold, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen; the executing of the breastplate, which bore the tribes' names on the heart, after the pattern — that the breastplate commanded is now made: "he made the breastplate of cunning work, like the work of the ephod; of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen"; the witnesses note the work, "of cunning work, like the work of the ephod" — the breastplate wrought with the same art and of the same precious materials as the ephod, made as commanded (28:15); the witnesses note that the breastplate was set with twelve stones, each graven with a tribe's name (vv.9-14), and worn on the high priest's breast; the witnesses note the great type before given, that, as the shoulder-stones (the place of strength) bore the tribes, so the breastplate (the place of the heart) bore them, shadowing Christ, our High Priest, who bears his people not only on his shoulders, in his strength, but on his heart, in his love; the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly; the witnesses note that we are borne both on the strength and on the love of our High Priest; so that the breastplate is made, like the ephod, after the pattern; and fine twined linen. The witnesses note And he made the breastplate of cunning work, like the work of the ephod. The witnesses note of gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen.

The witnesses note the breastplate made of cunning work like the ephod, of gold and the rich colours, worn on the high priest's breast — bearing the tribes on the heart as the shoulder-stones bore them in strength; a type of Christ our High Priest, who bears his people not only on his shoulders, in his strength, but on his heart, in his love.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the breastplate, which bore the tribes on the heart. The lesson the expositors draw is that the breastplate was made like the ephod, a type of Christ who bears his people on his heart, in his love, as on his shoulders, in his strength. The witnesses note the cunning work: "of cunning work, like the work of the ephod" — Clarke notes as commanded; the cunning work; the as commanded; the the same art; the the same materials; the the ephod. The expositors observe the on the breast: worn on the breast; the on the breast; the worn; the the heart; the twelve stones; the the names. The witnesses press the shoulders and heart: the shoulders and the heart; the shoulders and heart; the the shoulders; the the heart; the strength; the love. The expositors draw the lesson on the bears on his heart: Christ bears on his heart; the bears on his heart; the Christ; the bears; the his heart; the his love. The expositors note the answered the command: the work answered the command. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of gold blue and purple and scarlet and fine twined linen, and the breastplate. The verse thus records the breastplate — teaching that it was made like the ephod, a type of Christ who bears his people on his heart, in his love.
9:
"It was foursquare; they made the breastplate double: a span was the length thereof, and a span the breadth thereof, being doubled." The witnesses note the breastplate made foursquare and doubled, a span each way. Gill notes the breastplate was made exactly according to the directions given to Moses, foursquare and doubled, a span in length and breadth. The witnesses note the making of the breastplate foursquare, doubled, a span in length and breadth; the executing of the breastplate's form after the pattern, square, doubled, and strong — that the breastplate is made foursquare and double: "It was foursquare; they made the breastplate double: a span was the length thereof, and a span the breadth thereof, being doubled"; the witnesses note the form, "foursquare" — that the breastplate was square, equal in length and breadth, a span each way, as commanded (28:16); the witnesses note the doubling, "they made the breastplate double... being doubled" — that it was folded double, the stronger to bear the twelve precious stones and to endure; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the foursquare form may signify stability and perfection, and the doubling strength, the breastplate firm to bear the names of the tribes on the high priest's heart; the witnesses note the type before given, that the breastplate, square and strong, bearing the tribes, shadows Christ, our High Priest, who bears his people firmly and surely on his heart; the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly; so that the breastplate is made foursquare, doubled, and strong; a span the breadth thereof, being doubled. The witnesses note It was foursquare; they made the breastplate double. The witnesses note a span was the length thereof, and a span the breadth thereof, being doubled.

The witnesses note the breastplate made foursquare and folded double, a span each way, as commanded — the doubling for strength, to bear the twelve precious stones; some seeing the foursquare form signify stability and perfection, and the doubling strength; the breastplate, square and strong, shadowing Christ who bears his people firmly and surely on his heart.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the breastplate's form, square, doubled, and strong. The lesson the expositors draw is that the breastplate was made foursquare and double, strong to bear the tribes, a type of Christ who bears his people firmly on his heart. The witnesses note the foursquare: "It was foursquare" — Gill notes square; the foursquare; the square; the equal; the a span; the each way. The expositors observe the doubled: "they made the breastplate double" — folded double; the doubled; the folded double; the the stronger; the to bear; the the stones. The witnesses press the a span: "a span... length... breadth" — as commanded; the a span; the as commanded; the the length; the the breadth; the the form. The expositors draw the lesson on the stability and strength: stability and strength; the stability and strength; the stability; the perfection; the strength; the firm. The witnesses observe the bears firmly: Christ bears firmly; the bears firmly; the Christ; the bears; the firmly; the his heart. The expositors note the answered the command: the work answered the command; the answered the command; the answered; the the command; the the pattern; the wrought. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the It was foursquare they made the breastplate double, the a span was the length thereof and a span the breadth thereof being doubled, and the form. The verse thus records the form of the breastplate — teaching that it was made foursquare and double, strong to bear the tribes, a type of Christ who bears his people firmly.
10:
"And they set in it four rows of stones: the first row was a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle: this was the first row." The witnesses note the four rows of precious stones set in the breastplate, each row named. Adam Clarke notes they set in it four rows of stones, all these precious stones being particularly explained in the notes on Exodus 28:17. The witnesses note the setting of the four rows of stones in the breastplate, the first being a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle; the executing of the twelve precious stones, each for a tribe, after the pattern — that the stones are set in rows as commanded: "they set in it four rows of stones: the first row was a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle"; the witnesses note the rows, "four rows of stones" — twelve precious stones in all, three in each of four rows, as commanded (28:17); the witnesses note the first row named, "a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle," the several precious stones each set in its place; the witnesses note that each stone was engraved with a tribe's name, so that the high priest bore all the tribes, each as a several jewel, on his heart; the witnesses note the great type before given, that the twelve precious stones, each a tribe, shadow the saints, who are God's jewels (Mal. 3:17), each precious and distinct, all borne on the heart of their High Priest; the witnesses note that God's people are precious stones in his sight, set in order and dear to him; the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly; so that the twelve stones are set in the breastplate; this was the first row. The witnesses note And they set in it four rows of stones: the first row was a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle. The witnesses note this was the first row.

The witnesses note the four rows of twelve precious stones set in the breastplate, each engraved with a tribe's name — each a several jewel borne on the high priest's heart; the stones shadowing the saints, who are God's jewels (Malachi 3:17), each precious and distinct, all borne on the heart of their High Priest, dear to him.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the twelve precious stones, each for a tribe. The lesson the expositors draw is that the twelve stones were set in rows, each a tribe, a type of the saints, God's jewels, each precious and borne on the heart of their High Priest. The witnesses note the four rows: "four rows of stones" — Clarke notes twelve in all; the four rows; the twelve in all; the three each; the as commanded; the the rows. The expositors observe the first row named: "a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle" — each in its place; the first row named; the each in its place; the the several stones; the set; the the first. The witnesses press the each a tribe: each engraved with a tribe; the each a tribe; the each; the a tribe; the the names; the borne. The expositors draw the lesson on the God's jewels: the saints, God's jewels; the God's jewels; the the saints; the God's jewels; the precious; the distinct. The expositors note the dear to him: precious and dear to him. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this was the first row, and the stones. The verse thus records the twelve precious stones — teaching that each tribe was a several jewel borne on the heart, a type of the saints, God's jewels.
23:
"And there was an hole in the midst of the robe, as the hole of an habergeon, with a band round about the hole, that it should not rend." The witnesses note the hole in the midst of the robe, bound about that it should not rend. Adam Clarke notes the habergeon or hauberk was a small coat of mail, like a half shirt, made of small iron rings curiously united, which covered the neck and breast and resisted the stroke of a sword. The witnesses note the making of the hole in the midst of the robe, like the hole of a habergeon, with a band round about that it should not rend; the strong-bound opening of the robe, made firm so as not to tear — that the robe's neck-hole is bound that it should not rend: "there was an hole in the midst of the robe, as the hole of an habergeon, with a band round about the hole, that it should not rend"; the witnesses note the opening, "an hole in the midst of the robe" — the opening for the head, through which the robe was put on; the witnesses note the likeness, "as the hole of an habergeon" — like the neck-opening of "a small coat of mail," strong and firm, made as commanded (28:32); the witnesses note the binding, "with a band round about the hole, that it should not rend" — that the edge was bound and woven, that the robe might not tear; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the robe, strongly bound that it should not rend, may shadow the seamless and unrending righteousness of Christ, whole and entire, never to be torn; the witnesses note the contrast some mark, that Christ's seamless robe was not rent at his death (John 19:23-24); the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly; so that the robe's hole is bound that it should not rend; that it should not rend. The witnesses note And there was an hole in the midst of the robe, as the hole of an habergeon, with a band round about the hole. The witnesses note that it should not rend.

The witnesses note the hole in the midst of the robe, like the neck of a coat of mail, bound about that it should not rend — strong and firm; some seeing the robe, bound that it should not rend, shadow the seamless and unrending righteousness of Christ, whole and entire, his seamless robe not rent at his death.

So the witnesses make the verse the strong-bound opening of the robe, made firm so as not to tear. The lesson the expositors draw is that the neck-hole was bound that the robe might not rend, some seeing the seamless and unrending righteousness of Christ. The witnesses note the hole in the midst: "an hole in the midst of the robe" — Clarke notes the opening for the head; the hole in the midst; the for the head; the put on; the the opening; the the robe. The expositors observe the as a habergeon: "as the hole of an habergeon" — a coat of mail; the as a habergeon; the a coat of mail; the strong; the firm; the the neck. The witnesses press the should not rend: "that it should not rend" — bound; the not tear. The expositors draw the lesson on the unrending righteousness: the unrending righteousness of Christ; the unrending righteousness; the unrending; the the righteousness; the of Christ; the whole. The expositors note the answered the command: the work answered the command. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that it should not rend, and the robe. The verse thus records the bound opening of the robe — teaching that it was made firm so as not to tear, some seeing the unrending righteousness of Christ.
24:
"And they made upon the hems of the robe pomegranates of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and twined linen." The witnesses note the pomegranates of the rich colours made upon the hems of the robe. Charles Ellicott notes the pomegranates were of blue, and purple, and scarlet, twisted together, as before directed (Exodus 28:33). The witnesses note the making of pomegranates of blue, purple, scarlet, and twined linen upon the hems of the robe; the executing of the fruit-figures on the robe's skirts after the pattern — that the pomegranates are made on the hem: "they made upon the hems of the robe pomegranates of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and twined linen"; the witnesses note the pomegranates, "pomegranates of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and twined linen" — woven figures of the fruit, of the rich colours "twisted together," set round the skirts of the robe, as commanded (28:33); the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the pomegranate, full of seeds, signifies fruitfulness, and that these fruit-figures on the hem of the high priest's robe shadow the fruit of good works and graces that should adorn the walk of God's priests; the witnesses note that, set with the bells (v.25), the pomegranates (fruit) and the bells (sound) together signify fruit and sound, the life and the doctrine, the practice and the profession; the witnesses note that the making answered the command; the witnesses note that the priest's walk should be adorned with fruit; so that the pomegranates are made on the hem of the robe; and twined linen. The witnesses note And they made upon the hems of the robe pomegranates of blue, and purple, and scarlet. The witnesses note and twined linen.

The witnesses note the pomegranates of the rich colours made on the hems of the robe — woven figures of the fruit; some seeing the pomegranate, full of seeds, signify fruitfulness, the fruit-figures shadowing the good works and graces that should adorn the walk of God's priests, and (with the bells) fruit and sound, the life and the doctrine together.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the fruit-figures on the robe's skirts. The lesson the expositors draw is that the pomegranates on the hem signify the fruit of good works adorning the priest's walk, fruit and sound, life and doctrine, together. The witnesses note the pomegranates: "pomegranates of blue, and purple, and scarlet" — Ellicott notes twisted together; the pomegranates; the twisted together; the the fruit; the woven; the the colours. The expositors observe the on the hems: "upon the hems of the robe" — round the skirts; the on the hems; the round the skirts; the the robe; the set; the as commanded. The witnesses press the fruitfulness: the pomegranate, fruitfulness; the fruitfulness; the the pomegranate; the full of seeds; the fruit; the good works. The expositors draw the lesson on the adorn the walk: adorn the priest's walk; the adorn the walk; the adorn; the the walk; the the priests; the graces. The witnesses observe the fruit and sound: fruit and sound, life and doctrine; the fruit and sound; the fruit; the sound; the life; the doctrine. The expositors note the answered the command: the work answered the command; the answered the command; the answered; the the command; the the pattern; the wrought. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And they made upon the hems of the robe pomegranates of blue and purple and scarlet, the and twined linen, and the pomegranates. The verse thus records the pomegranates — teaching that they signify the fruit of good works adorning the priest's walk, fruit and sound together.
25:
"And they made bells of pure gold, and put the bells between the pomegranates upon the hem of the robe, round about between the pomegranates;" The witnesses note the bells of pure gold made and set between the pomegranates on the hem. Charles Ellicott notes the bells of pure gold were set between the pomegranates, on the object of which see the note on Exodus 28:35, that their sound might be heard when the high priest went in and out. The witnesses note the making of bells of pure gold, set between the pomegranates round about the hem of the robe; the executing of the golden bells, whose sound went before the high priest, after the pattern — that the bells are set between the fruit: "they made bells of pure gold, and put the bells between the pomegranates upon the hem of the robe, round about"; the witnesses note the bells, "bells of pure gold" — golden bells, set "between the pomegranates," alternating with the fruit round the hem, as commanded (28:33-35); the witnesses note the purpose, that their sound "might be heard when the high priest went in" to the holy place and came out, "that he die not"; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the bells (sound) and the pomegranates (fruit) together signify the sound of the gospel and the fruit of good works, the profession and the practice, the doctrine and the life, that should go together in God's ministers; the witnesses note that the sound of the bells, the testimony, was to be heard, and the fruit seen; the witnesses note that profession without fruit, or fruit without profession, is defective, but both together adorn the priest; the witnesses note that the making answered the command; so that the golden bells are set between the pomegranates; round about between the pomegranates. The witnesses note And they made bells of pure gold, and put the bells between the pomegranates upon the hem of the robe. The witnesses note round about between the pomegranates.

The witnesses note the golden bells set between the pomegranates round the hem, their sound heard when the high priest went in and out — some seeing the bells (sound) and pomegranates (fruit) signify the sound of the gospel and the fruit of good works, the profession and the practice, the doctrine and the life, which should go together in God's ministers.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the golden bells, whose sound went before the high priest. The lesson the expositors draw is that the bells, set between the fruit, signify the sound of testimony with the fruit of good works, profession and practice together, both adorning the priest. The witnesses note the bells of gold: "bells of pure gold" — Ellicott notes between the pomegranates; the bells of gold; the between the pomegranates; the golden; the set; the the hem. The expositors observe the their sound heard: "heard when the high priest went in" — and out; the their sound heard; the went in; the and out; the that he die not; the the sound. The witnesses press the sound and fruit: the bells, sound; the pomegranates, fruit; the sound and fruit; the the bells; the sound; the the fruit. The expositors draw the lesson on the profession and practice: profession and practice; the profession and practice; the profession; the practice; the the doctrine; the the life. The expositors note the answered the command: the work answered the command. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the round about between the pomegranates, and the bells. The verse thus records the golden bells — teaching that they signify the sound of testimony with the fruit of good works, profession and practice together.
27:
"And they made coats of fine linen of woven work for Aaron, and for his sons," The witnesses note the coats of fine linen made for Aaron and his sons. Charles Ellicott notes "they made coats," rather tunics or shirts, of fine linen, for Aaron and his sons, as before directed (Exodus 28:40). The witnesses note the making of the coats of fine linen, of woven work, for Aaron and his sons; the executing of the white linen tunics after the pattern, the inner garments of the priests — that the linen coats are made: "they made coats of fine linen of woven work for Aaron, and for his sons"; the witnesses note the coats, "coats of fine linen of woven work" — the tunics or shirts of fine white linen, woven, the inner garments worn by both the high priest and his sons; the witnesses note that these were for "Aaron, and for his sons," all the priests being clothed in linen; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the white fine linen signifies purity and righteousness, "the righteousness of saints" (Rev. 19:8), the priests clothed in white as a figure of the holiness needful to serve God; the witnesses note that all who minister to God must be clothed in the white linen of righteousness; the witnesses note that the making answered the command; the witnesses note that the priests, high and common, were alike clothed in linen, all needing the same righteousness; so that the linen coats are made for the priests; and for his sons. The witnesses note And they made coats of fine linen of woven work for Aaron. The witnesses note and for his sons.

The witnesses note the coats of fine white linen made for Aaron and his sons, the inner garments of the priests — some seeing the white fine linen signify purity and righteousness, "the righteousness of saints," the priests clothed in white as a figure of the holiness needful to serve God, all who minister to him clothed in the white linen of righteousness.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the white linen tunics. The lesson the expositors draw is that the linen coats were made for all the priests, a figure of the purity and righteousness needful to serve God, all clothed in the same white linen. The witnesses note the coats of fine linen: "coats of fine linen of woven work" — Ellicott notes tunics; the coats of fine linen; the tunics; the shirts; the woven; the the inner garments. The expositors observe the for Aaron and his sons: "for Aaron, and for his sons" — all the priests; the for Aaron and his sons; the all the priests; the high and common; the clothed; the in linen. The witnesses press the white linen: the white fine linen; the white linen; the white; the fine linen; the purity; the righteousness. The expositors draw the lesson on the righteousness of saints: "the righteousness of saints"; the righteousness of saints; the the righteousness; the of saints; the clothed; the in white. The witnesses observe the holiness needful: the holiness needful to serve; the holiness needful; the holiness; the needful; the to serve; the God. The expositors note the same righteousness: all needing the same righteousness; the same righteousness; the all; the the same; the righteousness; the clothed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And they made coats of fine linen of woven work for Aaron, the and for his sons, and the coats. The verse thus records the linen coats — teaching that they were made for all the priests, a figure of the righteousness needful to serve God.
28:
"And a mitre of fine linen, and goodly bonnets of fine linen, and linen breeches of fine twined linen," The witnesses note the mitre, bonnets, and linen breeches made of fine linen. Charles Ellicott notes the mitre, or rather turban, was for Aaron, the goodly bonnets, or caps, for his sons, and the linen breeches, or drawers, for both Aaron and his sons. The witnesses note the making of the mitre of fine linen for Aaron, the bonnets for his sons, and the linen breeches for all; the executing of the head-tires and the breeches after the pattern, the rest of the linen garments — that the mitre, bonnets, and breeches are made: "a mitre of fine linen, and goodly bonnets of fine linen, and linen breeches of fine twined linen"; the witnesses note the mitre, "a mitre of fine linen" (a "turban"), for Aaron the high priest; the witnesses note the bonnets, "goodly bonnets" (or "caps"), for his sons, the common priests; the witnesses note the breeches, "linen breeches of fine twined linen" (or "drawers"), made "both for Aaron and for his sons," to cover their nakedness in the service; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the linen breeches, covering nakedness, signify the need of covering for our shame in approaching God, and the modesty and purity required in his service; the witnesses note that all, from the head to the loins, was clothed in white linen, the figure of righteousness and purity; the witnesses note that the making answered the command; so that the mitre, bonnets, and breeches are made of linen; and linen breeches of fine twined linen. The witnesses note And a mitre of fine linen, and goodly bonnets of fine linen. The witnesses note and linen breeches of fine twined linen.

The witnesses note the mitre for Aaron, the bonnets for his sons, and the linen breeches for all, made of fine linen — some seeing the breeches, covering nakedness, signify the need of covering for our shame in approaching God and the purity required in his service; all, from head to loins, clothed in white linen, the figure of righteousness and purity.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the head-tires and breeches. The lesson the expositors draw is that the mitre, bonnets, and breeches were made of fine linen, all clothed in white from head to loins, a figure of righteousness and purity. The witnesses note the mitre: "a mitre of fine linen" — Ellicott notes a turban, for Aaron; the mitre; the a turban; the for Aaron; the the high priest; the the head. The expositors observe the bonnets: "goodly bonnets" — caps, for his sons; the bonnets; the caps; the for his sons; the the common priests; the the head. The witnesses press the breeches: "linen breeches" — drawers, for all; the breeches; the drawers; the for all; the cover nakedness; the the service. The expositors draw the lesson on the covering for shame: covering for our shame; the covering for shame; the covering; the our shame; the approaching God; the modesty. The witnesses observe the white from head to loins: white from head to loins; the white from head to loins; the white; the head to loins; the righteousness; the purity. The expositors note the answered the command: the work answered the command; the answered the command; the answered; the the command; the the pattern; the wrought. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And a mitre of fine linen and goodly bonnets of fine linen, the and linen breeches of fine twined linen, and the garments. The verse thus records the head-tires and breeches — teaching that all were made of fine linen, a figure of righteousness and purity.
29:
"And a girdle of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, of needlework; as the LORD commanded Moses." The witnesses note the girdle made of fine linen and the rich colours, of needlework, as the LORD commanded. Charles Ellicott notes the girdle was for Aaron, described much more fully here than in the instructions, where it is called simply a girdle of needlework (Exodus 28:39). The witnesses note the making of the girdle of fine twined linen and the three colours, of needlework, as the LORD commanded Moses; the executing of the girdle, the last of the priestly garments, after the pattern — that the girdle is made, and all as commanded: "a girdle of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, of needlework; as the LORD commanded Moses"; the witnesses note the girdle, "a girdle of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, of needlework" — the sash of fine linen and the rich colours, wrought with the needle, for Aaron; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the girdle, which binds and girds, signifies the readiness, strength, and diligence needful for the priestly service, the loins girt for the work (Luke 12:35); the witnesses note the refrain, "as the LORD commanded Moses" — that this, like all the rest, was made exactly as commanded; the witnesses note that the girdle, the last garment, completes the priestly attire, all wrought after the pattern; the witnesses note that the making of every garment answered the command; the witnesses note that God's servants are to be girt and ready for his service; so that the girdle is made, and all as commanded; as the LORD commanded Moses. The witnesses note And a girdle of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, of needlework. The witnesses note as the LORD commanded Moses.

The witnesses note the girdle made of fine linen and the rich colours, of needlework, as the LORD commanded — the last of the priestly garments; some seeing the girdle, which binds and girds, signify the readiness, strength, and diligence needful for the priestly service, the loins girt for the work, all wrought exactly after the pattern.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the girdle, the last of the priestly garments. The lesson the expositors draw is that the girdle was made as commanded, completing the attire, some seeing it signify the readiness and diligence needful for service, the loins girt. The witnesses note the the girdle: "a girdle of fine twined linen" — Ellicott notes for Aaron; the the girdle; the for Aaron; the the sash; the the colours; the of needlework. The expositors observe the of needlework: "of needlework" — wrought with the needle; the of needlework; the wrought; the the needle; the the colours; the the linen. The witnesses press the binds and girds: the girdle binds and girds; the binds and girds; the binds; the girds; the readiness; the strength. The expositors draw the lesson on the girt for the work: the loins girt for the work; the girt for the work; the the loins; the girt; the for the work; the diligence. The witnesses observe the as commanded: "as the LORD commanded Moses" — exactly; the as commanded; the exactly; the the refrain; the the pattern; the obeyed. The expositors note the completes the attire: the last garment completes the attire; the completes the attire; the the last; the completes; the the attire; the all wrought. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And a girdle of fine twined linen and blue and purple and scarlet of needlework, the as the LORD commanded Moses, and the girdle. The verse thus records the girdle — teaching that it was made as commanded, some seeing it signify the readiness and diligence needful for service.
30:
"And they made the plate of the holy crown of pure gold, and wrote upon it a writing, like to the engravings of a signet, HOLINESS TO THE LORD." The witnesses note the plate of the holy crown made of pure gold, engraved "HOLINESS TO THE LORD." Adam Clarke notes the holy crown of pure gold; on Asiatic and Egyptian monuments the priests are represented wearing crowns or tiaras, and the priests among the ancient Greeks were styled crown-bearers. The witnesses note the making of the plate of the holy crown of pure gold, engraved like a signet, "HOLINESS TO THE LORD"; the executing of the golden plate of holiness, the crown of the high priest, after the pattern — that the crown of holiness is made: "they made the plate of the holy crown of pure gold, and wrote upon it a writing, like to the engravings of a signet, HOLINESS TO THE LORD"; the witnesses note the plate, "the plate of the holy crown of pure gold" — the golden plate fastened on the front of the mitre, called the "holy crown," as commanded (28:36); the witnesses note the writing, "like to the engravings of a signet, HOLINESS TO THE LORD" — that these words were graven on it, as a seal is graven, marking the high priest as wholly consecrated to God; the witnesses note the great type before given, that the golden crown of holiness, worn on the forehead, shadows Christ, our High Priest, who is holiness itself, "Holiness to the LORD," and who, bearing the iniquity of the holy things (28:38), makes the worship and the worshippers accepted; the witnesses note that the holiness of Christ, the crown of his priesthood, covers the defects of our holy things, that they may be accepted; the witnesses note that the making answered the command; so that the golden crown of holiness is made; HOLINESS TO THE LORD. The witnesses note And they made the plate of the holy crown of pure gold, and wrote upon it a writing, like to the engravings of a signet. The witnesses note HOLINESS TO THE LORD.

The witnesses note the golden plate of the holy crown made and engraved, like a signet, "HOLINESS TO THE LORD" — worn on the high priest's forehead, marking him wholly consecrated; the great type of Christ our High Priest, who is holiness itself and, bearing the iniquity of the holy things, makes the worship and worshippers accepted, his holiness covering the defects of our holy things.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the golden plate of holiness, the crown of the high priest. The lesson the expositors draw is that the crown "HOLINESS TO THE LORD" was made, a type of Christ who is holiness itself and makes our holy things accepted. The witnesses note the the plate: "the plate of the holy crown of pure gold" — Clarke notes the crown; the the plate; the the crown; the pure gold; the the mitre; the the front. The expositors observe the like a signet: "like to the engravings of a signet" — graven; the like a signet; the graven; the as a seal; the the words; the deep. The witnesses press the HOLINESS TO THE LORD: "HOLINESS TO THE LORD" — wholly consecrated; the the forehead. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ holiness itself: Christ, holiness itself; the Christ holiness itself; the Christ; the holiness; the itself; the his crown. The expositors note the covers our defects: his holiness covers our defects. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the HOLINESS TO THE LORD, and the crown. The verse thus records the golden crown of holiness — teaching that it was made, a type of Christ who is holiness itself and makes our holy things accepted.
32:
"Thus was all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of the congregation finished: and the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses, so did they." The witnesses note all the work of the tabernacle finished, the children of Israel doing according to all the LORD commanded. Matthew Henry notes the builders made very good despatch, it being not much more than five months from the beginning to the finishing; many hands were employed, all unanimous, the workmen taught of God, and the people hearty and zealous; and they punctually observed their orders, not varying in the least. The witnesses note the whole work of the tabernacle finished, the children of Israel doing all according to all the LORD commanded Moses; the completing of the whole work in exact obedience — that all is finished, and all done as commanded: "Thus was all the work of the tabernacle... finished: and the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses"; the witnesses note the despatch, that "the builders made very good despatch," finishing the whole "in... five months," though it was fine and curious work; the witnesses note the exactness, "according to all that the LORD commanded Moses... so did they" — that they "punctually observed their orders, and did not in the least vary"; the witnesses note the great rule the commentators draw, that "God's work must be done... according to his own will," his institutions neither needing nor admitting "men's inventions," for "God is pleased with willing worship, but not with will-worship"; the witnesses note that the work of God is to be done exactly as he commands; so that all is finished, in exact obedience; so did they. The witnesses note Thus was all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of the congregation finished. The witnesses note and the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses, so did they.

The witnesses note all the work finished, done according to all the LORD commanded — with great despatch (many hands unanimous, the workmen taught of God, the people hearty and zealous), and with exact obedience; God's work must be done according to his own will, his institutions admitting no men's inventions, for God is pleased with willing worship but not with will-worship.

So the witnesses make the verse the completing of the whole work in exact obedience. The lesson the expositors draw is that all was finished as commanded, with despatch and exactness, God's work to be done according to his own will, not by man's invention. The witnesses note the all finished: "all the work... finished" — Henry notes good despatch; the all finished; the good despatch; the five months; the fine work; the suddenly. The expositors observe the many hands unanimous: "many hands... all unanimous" — taught of God; the many hands unanimous; the taught of God; the the workmen; the hearty; the zealous. The witnesses press the as commanded: "according to all that the LORD commanded" — punctually; the as commanded; the punctually; the not varying; the their orders; the so did they. The expositors draw the lesson on the his own will: God's work by his own will; the his own will; the God's work; the his own will; the no inventions; the not admit. The expositors note the add not: add not to his words. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses so did they, and the finishing. The verse thus records the completing of the work — teaching that all was finished in exact obedience, God's work to be done according to his own will.
33:
"And they brought the tabernacle unto Moses, the tent, and all his furniture, his taches, his boards, his bars, and his pillars, and his sockets," The witnesses note the people bringing the tabernacle and all its parts to Moses. Matthew Henry notes they brought all their work to Moses, and submitted it to his inspection; he knew what he had ordered them to make, and now the particulars were called over, that Moses might see they had made all, omitting nothing, and all according to the instructions. The witnesses note the people bringing the tabernacle, the tent, and all its furniture, taches, boards, bars, pillars, and sockets to Moses; the submitting of all the finished work to Moses for inspection — that they bring all and submit it to be examined: "they brought the tabernacle unto Moses, the tent, and all his furniture, his taches, his boards, his bars, and his pillars, and his sockets"; the witnesses note the bringing, "they brought the tabernacle unto Moses... and all his furniture" — that the several parts, before they were set up, were brought and "submitted... to his inspection"; the witnesses note the listing, "his taches, his boards, his bars, and his pillars, and his sockets" — that "the particulars were called over, and all produced," that Moses might see "they had made all, omitting nothing, and... according to the instructions"; the witnesses note that "the spirit of the prophets is subject to the prophets" (1 Cor. 14:32); the witnesses note that the gifted are to submit their work to lawful authority; so that all the work is brought and submitted to Moses; and his sockets. The witnesses note And they brought the tabernacle unto Moses, the tent, and all his furniture. The witnesses note his taches, his boards, his bars, and his pillars, and his sockets.

The witnesses note the people bringing all the finished work to Moses and submitting it to his inspection — that he might see they had made all, omitting nothing, and all according to the instructions; the humility that, though taught of God, they were willing to have their work examined and compared with the model, paying deference to lawful authority, the spirit of the prophets subject to the prophets.

So the witnesses make the verse the submitting of all the finished work to Moses. The lesson the expositors draw is that they brought all and submitted it to inspection, with humility, the gifted submitting their work to lawful authority. The witnesses note the brought to Moses: "brought the tabernacle unto Moses" — Henry notes submitted to inspection; the brought to Moses; the submitted; the to inspection; the the parts; the produced. The expositors observe the particulars called over: "the particulars were called over" — all produced; the particulars called over; the all produced; the omitting nothing; the the instructions; the examined. The witnesses press the made all: "they had made all" — nothing omitted; the made all; the nothing omitted; the according to the instructions; the all; the right. The expositors draw the lesson on the humility: humility to be examined; the humility; the humility; the to be examined; the the model; the deference. The expositors note the submit to authority: submit work to lawful authority. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the his taches his boards his bars and his pillars and his sockets, and the submitting. The verse thus records the submitting of the work — teaching that they brought all and submitted it to Moses, with humility, the gifted submitting to lawful authority.
34:
"And the covering of rams' skins dyed red, and the covering of badgers' skins, and the vail of the covering," The witnesses note the coverings and the veil brought among the things presented to Moses. Charles Ellicott notes the vail of the covering, that is, the vail which separated between the holy place and the holy of holies, covering the latter from the sight of man. The witnesses note the coverings of rams' skins dyed red and of badgers' skins, and the veil of the covering, brought to Moses among the finished work; the presenting of the coverings and the veil for inspection — that these too are brought and shown: "the covering of rams' skins dyed red, and the covering of badgers' skins, and the vail of the covering"; the witnesses note the coverings, "the covering of rams' skins dyed red, and... of badgers' skins" — the two outer coverings, brought with the rest; the witnesses note the veil, "the vail of the covering" — that is, "the vail which separated between the holy place and the holy of holies, covering the latter from the sight of man"; the witnesses note that all these parts, like the rest, were produced before Moses, that he might see all was made and made aright; the witnesses note the types before given, that the rams' and badgers' skins shadow Christ's atoning sufferings and lowly appearance, and the veil his flesh, dividing yet (under the law) the way to the holiest; the witnesses note that every part was brought and submitted, none kept back; the witnesses note that the whole was made according to the command; so that the coverings and veil are brought to Moses; and the vail of the covering. The witnesses note And the covering of rams' skins dyed red, and the covering of badgers' skins. The witnesses note and the vail of the covering.

The witnesses note the two outer coverings and the veil brought to Moses among the finished work — the veil that separated the holy place from the holy of holies, covering it from the sight of man; the rams' and badgers' skins shadowing Christ's atoning sufferings and lowly appearance, and the veil his flesh, all the parts brought and submitted, none kept back.

So the witnesses make the verse the presenting of the coverings and the veil for inspection. The lesson the expositors draw is that these too were produced before Moses, that he might see all was made aright, the types of Christ's sufferings and flesh brought with the rest. The witnesses note the coverings: "rams' skins... badgers' skins" — Ellicott notes the outer coverings; the coverings; the the outer coverings; the rams' skins; the badgers' skins; the brought. The expositors observe the the veil: "the vail of the covering" — the partition; the the veil; the the partition; the the holy place; the the holy of holies; the from man. The witnesses press the all produced: all produced before Moses; the all produced; the all; the produced; the before Moses; the shown. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ's sufferings: the skins, Christ's sufferings; the Christ's sufferings; the the skins; the atoning; the lowly; the appearance. The witnesses observe the the veil his flesh: the veil, his flesh; the the veil his flesh; the the veil; the his flesh; the dividing; the the way. The expositors note the none kept back: none kept back, all submitted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the vail of the covering, and the coverings. The verse thus records the coverings and veil brought — teaching that these too were produced before Moses, the types of Christ's sufferings and flesh among them.
37:
"The pure candlestick, with the lamps thereof, even with the lamps to be set in order, and all the vessels thereof, and the oil for light," The witnesses note the pure candlestick with its lamps and oil brought among the finished work. Adam Clarke notes the pure candlestick, the lamps to be set in order, that is, to be trimmed and fresh oiled every day, for the purpose of being lighted in the evening. The witnesses note the pure candlestick, with its lamps to be set in order, all its vessels, and the oil for light, brought to Moses; the presenting of the candlestick and its furniture for inspection — that the candlestick and its light are brought: "the pure candlestick, with the lamps thereof... and all the vessels thereof, and the oil for light"; the witnesses note the candlestick, "the pure candlestick, with the lamps thereof" — the golden candlestick of the holy place, with its seven lamps; the witnesses note the lamps, "the lamps to be set in order" — that is, "to be trimmed and fresh oiled every day," for "being lighted in the evening"; the witnesses note the vessels and oil, "all the vessels thereof, and the oil for light" — the snuffers and dishes, and the pure oil for the light; the witnesses note the type before given, that the candlestick, giving light in the holy place, shadows Christ the light of the world, and the church bearing his light, fed by the oil of the Spirit and tended daily; the witnesses note that the light must be daily trimmed and supplied, the believer's light kept burning by daily grace; the witnesses note that all was brought and shown to Moses; so that the candlestick and its light are brought; and the oil for light. The witnesses note The pure candlestick, with the lamps thereof, even with the lamps to be set in order, and all the vessels thereof. The witnesses note and the oil for light.

The witnesses note the pure candlestick with its lamps (to be trimmed and fresh oiled daily and lighted in the evening), its vessels, and the oil for light, brought to Moses — the candlestick shadowing Christ the light of the world, and the church bearing his light, fed by the oil of the Spirit and tended daily, the believer's light kept burning by daily grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the presenting of the candlestick and its furniture. The lesson the expositors draw is that the candlestick and its light were brought to Moses, a type of Christ the light and the church bearing his light, daily trimmed and supplied. The witnesses note the the candlestick: "the pure candlestick" — Clarke notes the golden candlestick; the the candlestick; the the golden candlestick; the the lamps; the the holy place; the seven. The expositors observe the set in order: "the lamps to be set in order" — trimmed daily; the set in order; the trimmed daily; the fresh oiled; the lighted; the the evening. The witnesses press the the oil: "the oil for light" — the pure oil; the the oil; the the pure oil; the the vessels; the the light; the supplied. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ the light: a type of Christ the light; the Christ the light; the Christ; the the light; the of the world; the the church. The expositors note the daily grace: kept burning by daily grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the oil for light, and the candlestick. The verse thus records the candlestick brought — teaching that it was shown to Moses, a type of Christ the light and the church bearing his light, daily trimmed.
38:
"And the golden altar, and the anointing oil, and the sweet incense, and the hanging for the tabernacle door," The witnesses note the golden altar, the anointing oil, the sweet incense, and the door hanging brought to Moses. Keil and Delitzsch note the golden altar, and the anointing oil, and the sweet incense, and the hanging for the tabernacle door, all brought among the finished work for Moses to see. The witnesses note the golden altar of incense, the anointing oil, the sweet incense, and the hanging for the door brought to Moses; the presenting of the incense-altar, oil, incense, and door for inspection — that these holy things are brought and shown: "the golden altar, and the anointing oil, and the sweet incense, and the hanging for the tabernacle door"; the witnesses note the golden altar, "the golden altar" — the altar of incense, of the holy place; the witnesses note the oil and incense, "the anointing oil, and the sweet incense" — the holy oil and the sweet incense, each of its appointed composition; the witnesses note the door, "the hanging for the tabernacle door" — the rich screen at the entrance; the witnesses note the types before given, that the golden altar and the sweet incense shadow the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession, a sweet savour before God; the anointing oil the Spirit; and the door hanging Christ the door, by whom we enter; the witnesses note that all these holy things were brought and produced before Moses, none wanting; the witnesses note that the whole was made according to the command; so that the golden altar, oil, incense, and door are brought; and the hanging for the tabernacle door. The witnesses note And the golden altar, and the anointing oil, and the sweet incense. The witnesses note and the hanging for the tabernacle door.

The witnesses note the golden altar of incense, the anointing oil, the sweet incense, and the door hanging brought to Moses among the finished work — the golden altar and sweet incense shadowing the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession, a sweet savour before God; the anointing oil the Spirit; and the door hanging Christ the door, all produced before Moses, none wanting.

So the witnesses make the verse the presenting of the incense-altar, oil, incense, and door. The lesson the expositors draw is that these holy things were brought to Moses, types of Christ's intercession, the Spirit, and Christ the door, all produced, none wanting. The witnesses note the golden altar: "the golden altar" — Keil notes the altar of incense; the golden altar; the the altar of incense; the the holy place; the brought; the shown. The expositors observe the oil and incense: "the anointing oil, and the sweet incense" — each appointed; the oil and incense; the each appointed; the the holy oil; the the incense; the composition. The witnesses press the the door: "the hanging for the tabernacle door" — the screen; the the door; the the screen; the the entrance; the rich; the brought. The expositors draw the lesson on the intercession: the altar and incense, intercession; the intercession; the the altar; the the incense; the the prayers; the a sweet savour. The expositors note the none wanting: all produced before Moses, none wanting. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the hanging for the tabernacle door, and the holy things. The verse thus records the holy things brought — teaching that the incense-altar, oil, incense, and door were produced before Moses, types of Christ's intercession, the Spirit, and Christ the door.
39:
"The brasen altar, and his grate of brass, his staves, and all his vessels, the laver and his foot," The witnesses note the brazen altar with its grate, staves, and vessels, and the laver, brought to Moses. Jamieson, Fausset and Brown note the tabernacle, from its name and appearance, was a tent, yet from the description, the boards that formed its walls, the bars that strengthened them, and the middle bar that held all, gave it strength and stability. The witnesses note the brazen altar with its grate of brass, staves, and vessels, and the laver and its foot, brought to Moses; the presenting of the vessels of the court for inspection — that the altar and laver are brought and shown: "the brasen altar, and his grate of brass, his staves, and all his vessels, the laver and his foot"; the witnesses note the brazen altar, "the brasen altar, and his grate of brass, his staves, and all his vessels" — the altar of burnt offering, with its grate, staves for carriage, and pans, shovels, and basins; the witnesses note the laver, "the laver and his foot" — the brazen laver, with its base, where the priests washed; the witnesses note the types before given, that the brazen altar shadows the cross of Christ, where sacrifice is made, and the laver the washing of regeneration and the word, the way to God being through sacrifice and cleansing; the witnesses note that these vessels of the court, the first met on entering, were brought and produced before Moses; the witnesses note that all was made according to the command; the witnesses note that, though the tabernacle was but a tent, it was given strength and stability by its frame; so that the brazen altar and the laver are brought; the laver and his foot. The witnesses note The brasen altar, and his grate of brass, his staves, and all his vessels. The witnesses note the laver and his foot.

The witnesses note the brazen altar with its grate, staves, and vessels, and the laver with its foot, brought to Moses — the vessels of the court; the brazen altar shadowing the cross of Christ where sacrifice is made, and the laver the washing of regeneration, the way to God through sacrifice and cleansing, all produced before Moses, the tabernacle though a tent given strength by its frame.

So the witnesses make the verse the presenting of the vessels of the court. The lesson the expositors draw is that the brazen altar and the laver were brought to Moses, types of the cross and the washing, the way to God through sacrifice and cleansing. The witnesses note the the brazen altar: "the brasen altar... his vessels" — JFB notes the altar of burnt offering; the the brazen altar; the the altar of burnt offering; the the grate; the the staves; the the vessels. The expositors observe the the laver: "the laver and his foot" — where they washed; the the laver; the where they washed; the the base; the brazen; the the priests. The witnesses press the the cross: the brazen altar, the cross; the the cross; the the brazen altar; the the cross; the sacrifice; the made. The expositors draw the lesson on the the washing: the laver, the washing; the the washing; the the laver; the regeneration; the the word; the cleansing. The expositors note the strength and stability: the frame gave strength and stability. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the laver and his foot, and the vessels. The verse thus records the vessels of the court brought — teaching that the altar and laver were produced before Moses, types of the cross and the washing.
41:
"The cloths of service to do service in the holy place, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and his sons' garments, to minister in the priest's office." The witnesses note the cloths of service and the holy garments of the priests brought to Moses. Keil and Delitzsch note the cloths of service to do service in the holy place, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and his sons' garments, to minister in the priest's office, brought among the finished work, all made according to all the LORD commanded. The witnesses note the cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron and his sons brought to Moses, to minister in the priest's office; the presenting of the priestly vestments, the last of the finished work, for inspection — that the garments too are brought and shown: "the cloths of service... and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and his sons' garments, to minister in the priest's office"; the witnesses note the cloths of service, "the cloths of service to do service in the holy place" — the cloths used in the ministry; the witnesses note the holy garments, "the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and his sons' garments" — all the priestly vestments, before described, "to minister in the priest's office"; the witnesses note that these, with all the rest, were brought and submitted to Moses, all made "according to all the LORD commanded"; the witnesses note the type before given, that the holy garments shadow the righteousness and graces with which Christ and his people are clothed for God's service; the witnesses note that all the work, garments and vessels alike, was now complete and produced; the witnesses note that nothing was wanting, all made as commanded; so that the priestly garments are brought to Moses; to minister in the priest's office. The witnesses note The cloths of service to do service in the holy place, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and his sons' garments. The witnesses note to minister in the priest's office.

The witnesses note the cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron and his sons brought to Moses, the last of the finished work — the holy garments shadowing the righteousness and graces with which Christ and his people are clothed for God's service; all the work, garments and vessels alike, now complete and produced, nothing wanting, all made as the LORD commanded.

So the witnesses make the verse the presenting of the priestly vestments, the last of the finished work. The lesson the expositors draw is that the garments too were brought and submitted to Moses, a type of being clothed with righteousness, all the work complete and produced. The witnesses note the cloths of service: "the cloths of service" — Keil notes for the ministry; the cloths of service; the for the ministry; the the holy place; the used; the brought. The expositors observe the holy garments: "the holy garments for Aaron... and his sons" — the vestments; the holy garments; the the vestments; the the high priest; the the sons; the to minister. The witnesses press the to minister: "to minister in the priest's office" — for service; the to minister; the for service; the the office; the the priests; the clothed. The expositors draw the lesson on the righteousness: a type of righteousness; the righteousness; the righteousness; the the graces; the clothed; the Christ. The expositors note the nothing wanting: nothing wanting, all as commanded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to minister in the priest's office, and the garments. The verse thus records the priestly garments brought — teaching that the vestments too were submitted to Moses, all the work complete, nothing wanting.
43:
"And Moses did look upon all the work, and, behold, they had done it as the LORD had commanded, even so had they done it: and Moses blessed them." The witnesses note Moses surveying all the work, finding it done as the LORD commanded, and blessing the people. Gill notes Moses made a survey of it, examined every particular with great care, and behold, they had done it as the LORD commanded, both as to matter and manner in every point, and Moses blessed them, commending them and imploring the divine blessing. The witnesses note Moses looking on all the work, finding it done exactly as the LORD commanded, and blessing the people; the inspection and blessing, the close of the work like the close of creation — that Moses surveys all, finds it right, and blesses: "Moses did look upon all the work, and, behold, they had done it as the LORD had commanded... and Moses blessed them"; the witnesses note the survey, "Moses did look upon all the work" — that he, the "general superintendent," "examined every particular thing... with great care and accuracy"; the witnesses note the verdict, "behold, they had done it as the LORD had commanded, even so had they done it" — that all was done "both as to matter and manner... just as they were ordered," for they had been "filled by the Spirit of God"; the witnesses note the blessing, "Moses blessed them" — that he "commended them" and "implored the divine blessing" on the people who had given and the workmen who had wrought, the Targum's form being, "may the Shechinah of the LORD dwell in the works of your hands"; the witnesses note that, as Moses surveyed all and blessed, so the close of God's work is his approval and blessing, like the close of creation (Gen. 2:1-3); so that Moses blesses the finished work; and Moses blessed them. The witnesses note Moses did look upon all the work, and, behold, they had done it as the LORD had commanded, even so had they done it. The witnesses note and Moses blessed them.

The witnesses note Moses surveying all the work, finding it done exactly as the LORD commanded, and blessing the givers and the workmen ("may the Shechinah of the LORD dwell in the works of your hands") — the tabernacle, on which God's glory rested, typifying the human nature of Christ, in whom "the Word was made flesh and dwelt (tabernacled) among us, full of grace and truth," the fulness of the Godhead dwelling in him.

So the witnesses make the verse the inspection and blessing, the close of the work like the close of creation. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses surveyed all, found it done as commanded, and blessed the people, the tabernacle typifying the human nature of Christ. The witnesses note the look upon all: "Moses did look upon all the work" — Gill notes great care; the look upon all; the great care; the examined; the every particular; the the superintendent. The expositors observe the as commanded: "as the LORD had commanded" — matter and manner; the as commanded; the matter and manner; the just as ordered; the the Spirit; the done. The witnesses press the blessed them: "Moses blessed them" — commended and prayed; the the workmen. The expositors draw the lesson on the the Shechinah dwell: "the Shechinah dwell in the works of your hands." The expositors note the the Word made flesh: "the Word was made flesh and tabernacled among us." The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and Moses blessed them, and the blessing. The verse thus records the inspection and blessing — teaching that Moses found all done as commanded and blessed them, the tabernacle typifying the human nature of Christ.
